original_hypothesis.title: USD Price Feed Distortion via Single Price Array Application
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function takes an array of token contracts but only a single USD price. When executed, this single price is applied to all tokens in the array, potentially causing massive mispricing if tokens with different values are bundled.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If operators attempt to update WETH and USDC in the same proposal, one will be drastically mispriced, distorting the getLockedWeightedValue calculation which drives protocol rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: A single proposedPrice is applied in a loop to all tokenContract addresses in the proposal array

preconditions: Price feed operators propose a price update for multiple tokens with different actual USD values in a single transaction

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious proposal bundles high-value and low-value tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users holding the artificially inflated token receive vastly disproportionate rewards

economic_questions: How does getLockedWeightedValue impact the downstream schnibbles emission?
Can operators be tricked into bundling tokens?
validation_requirements: Simulate a bundled proposal and measure the impact on getLockedWeightedValue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Existing locked positions in the mispriced token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: None for the user (relies on operator action)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Thousands of times normal reward rate (e.g., if USDC is priced at WETH value)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until a new price proposal corrects it

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe distortion of protocol economy and unfair reward distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Holders of the correctly priced or undervalued tokens

validation_result.mechanism: single price applied to multiple tokens
validation_result.impact_summary: The USD price update mechanism applies a single proposedPrice to all token contracts in the proposal array, making it impossible to correctly price tokens with different USD values and potentially causing massive mispricing that distorts reward distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a structural code defect in the USD price update mechanism. The `proposeUSDPrice` function accepts a single `uint256 _price` and an `address[] _contracts` array. When the proposal reaches the approval threshold and `_execUSDPriceUpdate` is called, that single price is applied in a loop to every token contract in the array. Since tokens like WETH (~$3000) and USDC (~$1) have vastly different USD values, bundling them in a single proposal would assign an incorrect price to at least one of them. The deployment test script (`configure-token-test.ts`) confirms that the system is intended to support tokens with different prices (USDB at $1, WETH at $3000, ETH at $3000). Additionally, only one proposal can be active at a time (enforced by `if (usdUpdateProposal.proposer != address(0)) revert ProposalInProgressError()`), meaning operators must serialize price updates, increasing the window of stale prices. The code lacks any per-token price mapping in the proposal struct, making this a clear design defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is directly visible in two locations: (1) `proposeUSDPrice` stores a single price for multiple contracts, and (2) `_execUSDPriceUpdate` iterates over all contracts and assigns the same single price to each. The deployment test script confirms tokens are configured with different USD prices, proving the system expects per-token pricing but the update mechanism cannot deliver it correctly for heterogeneous token sets.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Accepts a single price for an array of token contracts. There is no mechanism to specify different prices for different tokens within a single proposal.
The single proposedPrice is applied identically to every token contract in the proposal array. If tokens have different actual USD values, all but one will be mispriced.

This is the downstream consumer of usdPrice. If a token is mispriced due to the single-price proposal mechanism, the locked weighted value calculation will be severely distorted, affecting reward distribution.

calling_sequence: 1. PriceFeed operator calls proposeUSDPrice(_price, [WETH_addr, USDC_addr]) with a single price
3. Other operators call approveUSDPrice(_price) to reach APPROVE_THRESHOLD
5. _execUSDPriceUpdate loops through contracts array applying proposedPrice to each: configuredTokens[WETH_addr].usdPrice = _price AND configuredTokens[USDC_addr].usdPrice = _price
6. getLockedWeightedValue uses the mispriced usdPrice values for reward weight calculation
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from proposeUSDPrice through approveUSDPrice to _execUSDPriceUpdate demonstrates that a single proposedPrice is stored in the proposal struct and then applied uniformly to all token addresses in the contracts array. At no point is there a per-token price mapping. The struct `USDUpdateProposal` contains `proposedPrice` (singular uint256) and `contracts` (address[]), making it architecturally impossible to set different prices for different tokens in one proposal.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Tokens configured with different USD prices
config_validation.deployment_evidence: configure-token-test.ts explicitly shows USDB configured at usdPrice: BigInt(1e18) ($1) while WETH is configured at usdPrice: BigInt(3000e18) ($3000). This confirms the system manages tokens with vastly different USD values, making the single-price proposal mechanism a clear defect.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario does not require a sophisticated attack. It can occur through operator error (bundling tokens with different values in a single proposal) or through a malicious/compromised operator intentionally bundling them to distort prices. Since the protocol manages tokens with 3000x price differences (ETH vs USDB), even a well-intentioned operator could make this mistake. The impact flows directly through getLockedWeightedValue to reward calculations, distorting the entire protocol economy. The single-proposal-at-a-time constraint (ProposalInProgressError) means correcting the error requires going through another full multisig cycle.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If WETH ($3000) and USDC ($1) are bundled in a single proposal: setting price to $3000 would make USDC holders appear 3000x wealthier in weighted value calculations; setting price to $1 would make WETH holders appear 3000x poorer. This directly impacts schnibbles emission rates and reward distribution through getLockedWeightedValue. The distortion persists until a new proposal corrects it, during which time rewards are unfairly distributed. The code also allows only one active proposal at a time, meaning correcting the mispricing for one token delays updates for others.
recommended_mitigations: Modify proposeUSDPrice to accept an array of prices (uint256[] _prices) corresponding to the array of contracts, and store them in a mapping within the proposal struct
In _execUSDPriceUpdate, apply each price from the array to its corresponding token contract
Alternatively, restrict proposals to a single token contract per proposal to eliminate the bundling risk entirely
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager and configure two tokens: TokenA (WETH-like, usdPrice=$3000) and TokenB (USDC-like, usdPrice=$1). 2. Have users lock equal dollar amounts of both tokens. 3. Call proposeUSDPrice($3000, [TokenA, TokenB]). 4. Have operators approve until threshold is met. 5. Verify both tokens now have usdPrice=$3000. 6. Call getLockedWeightedValue for a user with TokenB locked and observe the 3000x inflation in weighted value.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol team considers per-proposal single pricing 'by design' with the expectation that operators never bundle tokens with different prices — though this would still be a risky design given the lack of validation
The exact downstream impact on schnibbles emission depends on external AccountManager logic not in scope
additional_testing_needed: Verify the USDUpdateProposal struct definition to confirm there is no per-token price mapping (only a single proposedPrice field)
Test the full reward calculation path from getLockedWeightedValue through AccountManager to quantify the actual schnibbles impact of mispricing
